coredata.dc:title: A dual-channel and dual-signal microfluidic paper chip for simultaneous rapid detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb
coredata.dc:creator: Hao, Guoying
Tian, Hongye
Zhang, Zhong
Qin, Xiaoxiao
Yang, Tian
Yuan, Li
Yang, Xingbin
coredata.dc:description: Using L-cysteine (L-Cys) modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) as fluorescence signals, we developed a novel dual-channel and dual-signal microfluidic paper chip, which enables portable and rapid analysis of difenoconazole and mancozeb by internal filter effect (IFE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET), respectively. The results indicate that the two single-fluorescence imprinted paper chips have great stability and fluorescence property, and the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip composed of them can achieve simultaneous rapid detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb in a mixed solution. The rapid quantitative detection of two templates was achieved under the same excitation wavelength (360 nm) within 5 min. The limits of detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb were 0.18 μg/mL and 2 μg/mL, respectively. Furthermore, the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip also had good specific recognition for their analogs. Moreover, its recoveries of the actual samples were 102.2–108.8% and 96.0–105.5%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations were all less than 5.6%. In conclusion, this work has successfully prepared a multi-template rapid quantitative pesticide residue detection sensor, which provides a useful exploration for the development of new universal rapid detection technologies and theories and is of great significance for the rapid detection of pesticide residues in export products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pesticide residues
Rapid detection
Dual-channel detection
Microfluidic paper chip
Quantum dots

Characterization of the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip
Applications of dual-channel microfluidic paper chip
Basic properties of CdTe QDs and CQDs
Dual-channel and dual-signal microfluidic paper chip
Fluorescence performance of imprinted Paper chips
Sensitivity and selectivity of Dual-channel microfluidic Paper chip
Practical applications of Dual-channel microfluidic Paper chip

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by research grants including the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31701705 and 31972183), the Shaanxi Province Key Research and Development Project (2021NY-145), the Xi'an Advanced Manufacturing Technology and Key Project (21XJZZ0008), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFF1102200), and the Fundamental Research Fund of the Central University (GK202206038).

This work was funded by research grants including the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31701705 and 31972183 ), the Shaanxi Province Key Research and Development Project ( 2021NY-145 ), the Xi’an Advanced Manufacturing Technology and Key Project ( 21XJZZ0008 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFF1102200), and the Fundamental Research Fund of the Central University (GK202206038).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Paper@CQDs@MIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs; (B) FT-IR spectra of (a) paper base, (b) Paper@CQDs@MIPs and (c) Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs; The TG and DTG curve of (C) Paper@CQDs@MIPs (D) Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs.

Laser Confocal Imaging: (A-E) responses of Paper@CQDs@MIPs to difenecycloazole of different concentrations; (F-J) responses of Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs. to different concentrations of mancozeb (The concentration from left to right was 0, 0.5, 1, 4, 10 μg/mL).

(A) Fluorescence lifetime of CQDs before and after exposure to difenoconazole; (B) Ultraviolet absorption spectrum of CQDs before and after exposure to difenoconazole; (C) Spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of difenoconazole and emission spectra of CQDs; (D) Fluorescence lifetime of CdTe QDs before and after exposure to mancozeb; (E) Ultraviolet absorption spectrum of CdTe QDs before and after exposure to mancozeb; (F) Spectral overlap between the absorption spectra of mancozeb and emission spectra of CdTe QDs. (G) HOMO and LUMO of difenoconazole, folic acid, mancozeb and thioglycolic acid.

Detection response of microfluidic paper chip: (A) response of Paper@CQDs@MIPs to template solutions of different concentrations; (B) responses of Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs to template solutions with different concentrations; linear curve of (C) Paper@CQDs@MIPs and (D) Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs concentration response; (E) Selectivity of Paper@CQDs@MIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs towards to other analogs (mancozeb, difenoconazole, azaconazole, propiconazole and carbendazol).
Spiked recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSD, %, n = 3) for the determination of difenoconazole and mancozeb in cucumber samples.

head.ce:title: A dual-channel and dual-signal microfluidic paper chip for simultaneous rapid detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shaanxi Engineering Laboratory for Food Green Processing and Safety Control, Engineering Research Center of High Value Utilization of Western Fruit Resources, Ministry of Education, College of Food Engineering and Nutritional Science, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Engineering Laboratory for Food Green Processing and Safety Control
Engineering Research Center of High Value Utilization of Western Fruit Resources
College of Food Engineering and Nutritional Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shaanxi Engineering Laboratory for Food Green Processing and Safety Control, Engineering Research Center of High Value Utilization of Western Fruit Resources, Ministry of Education, College of Food Engineering and Nutritional Science, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710062, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xi’an Key Laboratory of Characteristic Fruit Storage and Preservation, Shaanxi Key Laboratory for Hazard Factors Assessment in Processing and Storage of Agricultural Products, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710062, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Xi’an Key Laboratory of Characteristic Fruit Storage and Preservation
Factors Assessment in Processing and Storage of Agricultural Products

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xi’an Key Laboratory of Characteristic Fruit Storage and Preservation, Shaanxi Key Laboratory for Hazard Factors Assessment in Processing and Storage of Agricultural Products, Shaanxi Normal University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710062, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using L-cysteine (L-Cys) modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) as fluorescence signals, we developed a novel dual-channel and dual-signal microfluidic paper chip, which enables portable and rapid analysis of difenoconazole and mancozeb by internal filter effect (IFE) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET), respectively. The results indicate that the two single-fluorescence imprinted paper chips have great stability and fluorescence property, and the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip composed of them can achieve simultaneous rapid detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb in a mixed solution. The rapid quantitative detection of two templates was achieved under the same excitation wavelength (360 nm) within 5 min. The limits of detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb were 0.18 μg/mL and 2 μg/mL, respectively. Furthermore, the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip also had good specific recognition for their analogs. Moreover, its recoveries of the actual samples were 102.2–108.8% and 96.0–105.5%, respectively, and the relative standard deviations were all less than 5.6%. In conclusion, this work has successfully prepared a multi-template rapid quantitative pesticide residue detection sensor, which provides a useful exploration for the development of new universal rapid detection technologies and theories and is of great significance for the rapid detection of pesticide residues in export products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, as the public pays more and more attention to health and food quality and safety, Pesticide residues in food are more and more concerned [1,2]. In production activities, in order to ensure the good appearance and economic benefits of agricultural products and their processed products, a variety of pesticides or plant hormones are often mixed to prevent and control the pests and diseases in agriculture and living environment. The direct exposure or residue in food and drinking water is easy to cause a variety of pesticide residues [3,4].

Mancozeb is an ethylene bisdithiocarbamate (EBDC) pesticide with a broad spectrum as an antifungal agent. Its principle of action is to inactivate amino acid sulfhydryl groups in fungal enzymes, hindering lipid metabolism, respiration and ATP production [5]. Therefore, it is widely used to prevent and control diseases caused by fungi in fruits, vegetables, and crops in order to achieve the purpose of increasing production. It is also an important inspection item of customs for exported food and agricultural products. Mancozeb enters the human body mainly through the skin and mouth, and its metabolites, ethyl thiourea, are harmful to the human body [6,7]. Several studies have confirmed that mancozeb has thyroid, liver, kidney, reproductive organs, nervous system, and gastrointestinal toxicity, as well as carcinogenic and teratogenic effects [8,9].

Difenoconazole is a triazole pesticide and a broad-spectrum fungicide used for disease prevention and control of vegetables, fruits, grains and other farmland crops [10]. Its bactericidal principle is to inhibit demethylation during the synthesis of ergosterol and destroy the structure and function of cell membranes to achieve disease prevention and treatment. Difenoconazole has been proved to have hepatotoxicity to juvenile and adult zebrafish [11]. Hence, excessive difenoconazole residues will not only cause environmental pollution, but also threaten human health.

At present, the detection methods of pesticide residues mainly include high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [12,13], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS) [14], and Surface-Enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS), etc. [15-17]. Due to problems of expensive equipment, long detection cycle and high professional requirements, these methods are unlikely to adapte to the characteristics of fast time or short shelf life in the food circulation process [18]. In addition, the actual sample matrix is complex and difficult to separate and purify. For this reason, it is of great practical value to develop a simple, low-cost, multi-channel fast food safety detection system [19].

The microfluidic paper chip is an integrated analysis and detection process on paper, which can realize the preliminary filtration and detection of complex samples [20]. It is characterized by abundant source, low cost, good biocompatibility, large specific surface area, low detection background, environmentally friendly sample transportation with self-porous structure [21]. Additionally, Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) can produce molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) with multiple specific binding sites [22], which can greatly improve the sensitivity and selectivity of detection, and are widely used in food safety, medical detection, environmental monitoring, microbial detection, and other fields due to their good recognition characteristics and stability [23-25]. Also, quantum dots (QDs), as a fluorescent sensor, have the advantages of good light stability, bright photoluminescence, high quantum yield, narrow emission, wide excitation, long fluorescence lifetime, etc. They have been widely used in metal ions, small molecules, and even in the analysis of biological macromolecules [26-30]. The effective combination of MIT and QDs-based microfluidic paper chips will greatly improve the detection performance of rapid detection sensors.

To assure food safety, this experiment used low-cost environmentally friendly microfluidic paper chips, combined with QDs fluorescence analysis based on MIT, to construct a dual-signal rapid detection sensor for the above two pesticides. By synthesizing CdTe quantum dots (CdTe QDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with two different wavelengths of fluorescent materials as fluorescent signals, we carried out the detection experiment and investigated the basic properties of these two QDs. Then QDs were grafted onto cellulose paper to prepare fluorescent sensing paper substrates Paper@CQDs and Paper@CdTe QDs. Finally, CdTe QDs imprinted paper chips (Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs) and CQDs imprinted paper chips (Paper@CQDs@MIPs) were prepared by depositing the imprinted polymer layer on the paper using difenoconazole and mancozeb as templates respectively. Thereby a dual-channel microfluidic paper chip was constructed, and a series of characterizations were further used to verify the performance of the rapid detection sensor. The sensor was used for the simultaneous rapid detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb and aimed to provide a theoretical basis for the rapid detection method of simultaneous detection of multiple pesticide residues in fruits and vegetables. This provides a useful exploration for the development of new universal rapid detection technologies and theories.

Synthesis and modification of CQDs: Refer to the previously reported method, which we modified [31]. 0.1 g of folic acid and 30 mL of ultra-pure water were added to the reactor vessel, which was sonicated for 10 min to make it thoroughly mixed, heated in an oven at 180 ℃ for 2 h, cooled to room temperature, and then taken out Supernatant. First, it was filtered with 0.22 μm membrane, and then G-25 Sephadex column was used for further purification and filtration to obtain CQDs. Finally, in order to enhance the stability of CQDs, the purified CQDs and L-Cys solution were mixed in a ratio of 5:7 and incubated at 37℃ for 40 min to prepare the modified quantum dots. It was stored in the dark at 4℃.

Synthesis of CdTe QDs: According to the previously reported means [32,33], quantum dots with different wavelengths were prepared by controlling the reflow time according to the characteristics of CdTe QDs. Accordingly, the reflux time in this experiment was set to 6 h to prepare orange fluorescent CdTe QDs with a longer wavelength. It was then stored in the dark at 4℃.

Determination of basic properties of QDs: Fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi) and laser particle size analyzer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern) were used to measure the fluorescence performance, particle size, and potential of the two QDs. The quantum yield (C9920-02G, Hamamatsu) and fluorescence storage stability of the two QDs were measured, respectively. The UV absorption spectra of the two QDs were determined using absorbance microplate reader (SpectraMax ABS Plus, Molecular Sensors). And the transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh, United Kingdom) was used to measure the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of CQDs and CdTe QDs.

Referring to the previously reference [34], we carried out solution preparation, paper acidification and fluorescence grafting. The acidified paper was shaken for 2 h by adding 200 μL APTES into 20 mL of 50% ethanol solution, and later, the CQDs and CdTe QDs were grafted to make Paper@CdTe QDs and Paper@CQDs. The prepared Paper@QDs were shaken with 20 mL ethanol and 50 μL TEOS for 4 h to achieve silanization.

Combined with MIT, difenoconazole was added into Paper@CQDs as the template molecule, and mancozeb was used as the template molecule in Paper@CdTe QDs. Then 50 μL APTES, 100 μL ammonia, and 50 μL TEOS was added, and shaking was continued for 4 h to make them fully combined. After rinsing with ultra-pure water three times, 10 mL 1% Tween 80 solution elution template was added for 20 min and rinsed with water repeatedly again to obtain Paper@CQDs@MIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs. In the meantime, the above steps were repeated except for adding the template to obtain Paper@CQDs@NIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@NIPs. All paper chips were stored at 4℃ in the dark. The fluorescence intensity stability of CQDs and CdTe QDs was continuously monitored for one month.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previously reported reference [35], wax-jet printing technology was used to prepare the paper chip carrier at 150℃ for 30 s. An external microfluidic channel was adopted, and a transparent hydrophobic film was used to fix the microfluidic channel and the Paper@QDs@MIPs. The sensor was designed as a dual-channel to simultaneously detect two templates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis and characterization of paper chip for rapid optical detection: The paper chips prepared above were characterized using environmental scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta 200, FEI company) and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, Tensor27, Brook, Germany) to verify the effectiveness of the synthesis process.

Determination of fluorescence thermal stability: The thermal analysis system (TG, Q1000DSC + LNCS + FACS Q600SDT, American TA company) was used to determine the thermal stability of the paper chip, and the data obtained were fitted for processing. The fluorescence emission spectra during the experiment were all measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Determination of response time: Similarly, under excitation of 360 nm, two template solutions were added to Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs respectively, to perform the above determination, and the response status and response time of Paper@CQDs@MIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs to different templates were observed.

Determination of concentration response of imprinted template: The laser confocal microscope (FV1200, Olympus) was used to carry out titration experiments with different concentrations to verify the concentration response effects of Paper@CQDs@MIPs on difenoconazole and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs on mancozeb respectively. The concentration of the template added was 0, 0.5, 1, 4, and 10 μg/mL, and the reaction time was 5 min.

Determination of concentration response of mixed template solution: The 10 μg/mL mancozeb solution was used as the solvent to prepare a certain concentration gradient difenoconazole solution. The concentration gradient was 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 μg/mL. The same is true for the configuration of mancozeb solution with difenoconazole solution as solvent, after which the mixed template solutions of different concentrations were dropped onto the paper chip. Paper@QDs@MIPs/NIPs were performed separately, and the reaction time was 5 min. The fluorescence titration spectrum of the Paper@QDs@MIPs was measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer to determine the response of the paper chip in the mixed pesticide.

Determination of selectivity: Three pesticides with similar structures, azaconazole, propiconazole, and carbendazol, with concentrations of 6 μg/mL, were prepared for selective investigation. 25 μL of the analog solution was added to Paper@QDs@MIPs, and 6 μg/mL of difenoconazole and mancozeb were used as references. The fluorescence intensity was measured on a fluorescence spectrophotometer after 10 min of reaction at 360 nm excitation wavelength.

During the detection process, the fluorescence lifetime and UV absorption spectrum of QDs before and after exposure to the substances to be tested, the UV absorption spectrum of the substances to be tested, and the fluorescence emission spectrum of QDs were measured to determine the mechanism of fluorescence quenching caused by the interaction between CQDs and CdTe QDs with difenoconazole and mancozeb, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of dual-channel microfluidic paper chip
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application of the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip in the detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb was determined in cucumber samples purchased from China Resources Vanguard Supermarket (Xi'an).

Treatment of cucumber samples spiked with difenoconazole: According to ISO standard method with slight improvement [36], 5 g cucumber samples were accurately weighed, mashed, and placed in a 50 mL conical flask, then 25 mL ethyl acetate and 7.5 g anhydrous sodium sulfate were added. The mixture was reacted in an oscillator for 20 min and filtered into a 50 mL concentrated flask. Subsequently, 10 mL ethyl acetate was added for repeated extraction once, and the extract was merged and concentrated to dry at 40℃ under reduced pressure. After dissolving in 2.5 mL ethanol, the simulated samples were prepared by adding 0, 4, 6, and 8 μg/mL of difenoconazole solutions, respectively.

Treatment of cucumber samples spiked with mancozeb: According to previous work [37], 5 g cucumber samples were pounded and digested with 2.5 mL 1:1 HCl. Then 2.5 mL methylene chloride was added to extract the samples. The extract was filtered and collected, concentrated until dry under reduced pressure, and dissolved with 2.5 mL ethanol before testing. The simulated samples were prepared by adding 0, 4, 6, and 8 μg/mL of mancozeb, respectively.

In order to confirm and evaluate the practicability of the method, 25 μL of simulated samples were added to the dual-channel microfluidic paper chip, respectively, and measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer. At the same time, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was used to compare the same samples [12,36].

Detection conditions for difenoconazole: Chromatographic column: ODSHypersil C18, mobile phase: methanol: acetonitrile: water = 35:53:30, column temperature: 25℃, flow rate: 1.0 mL/min, detection wavelength: 235 nm, isocratic elution.

Detection conditions for mancozeb: Column: ODS Hypersil C18, mobile phase: acetonitrile: 0.5% phosphoric acid water = 45:55, column temperature: 25℃, flow rate: 1.0 mL/min, detection wavelength: 272 nm, isocratic elution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Basic properties of CdTe QDs and CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare two kinds of microfluidic paper chip with single fluorescence signal, this experiment first synthesized two fluorescent substances, CQDs and CdTe QDs. CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method using folic acid as the carbon source, and modified with L-Cys to improve its fluorescence stability [31], We determined that the particle size of CQDs was about 7.44 nm, and they were negatively charged, with a potential of −21.6 mV and a quantum yield of 15.9%. The maximum excitation wavelength was around 360 nm, and there was maximum emission at 450 nm (Fig. S1 A). As shown in the upper right corner of Fig. S1A, the CQDs were pale green and close to colorless under fluorescent lamps, and strong blue fluorescent under ultraviolet light. The orange CdTe QDs had a particle size of about 8.66 nm and were also negatively charged. Its potential was close to CQDs at −24.2 mV, and the quantum yield was high, which could reach 32%. It had a maximum emission at a longer wavelength of 600 nm, thus, showed bright orange fluorescence under ultraviolet light (Fig. S1B).

The absorption peak of the first exciton of CQDs and CdTe QDs was located at 333 nm and 294 nm, respectively. And the spectral band gap E was about 3.72 eV and 4.22 eV from formula 1, respectively.
(1)
E = h v = h c / λ
= 1240 / λ
where, E is the spectral band gap (eV), h is Planck's constant (h ≈ 6.626 × 10−34, J·s), v is frequency (1/s), c is the speed of light (c ≈ 2.998 × 1017, nm/s), and λ is the wavelength (nm).

Additionally, we also continuously monitored the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and CdTe QDs during a one-month storage period protected from light. The results revealed that CQDs and CdTe QDs were easy to store and had good fluorescence stability under appropriate conditions (Fig. S2 A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channel and dual-signal microfluidic paper chip
ce:section.ce:para: The key part of the sensor was two Paper@QDs@MIPs. Firstly, we prepared Paper@QDs@MIPs/NIPs according to the steps shown in Fig. S3 as mentioned above (taking CQDs as an example). The same applied to Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs/NIPs.

According to the requirements, we designed a dual-channel microfluidic paper chip, as shown in Fig. 1. The circular area in the middle was the sample application area, the square areas on either side were the Paper@QDs@MIPs prepared in this experiment, and the rectangular areas connecting the two sides were the microfluidic channels.

In this work, an external microfluidic channel was prepared by a direct cutting method, and then a hollow transparent hydrophobic film was used to fix the microfluidic channel and Paper@QDs@MIPs on a completely hydrophobic substrate. The sample application area and the microfluidic channel were both hydrophilic areas composed of cellulose paper, which was of low cost, allowed microfluidic diffusion and transportation, and impurity removal (mainly insoluble impurities) [38]. In the actual detection process, after dropping an appropriate amount of the solution to be tested in the sample application area and the capillary force of the cellulose paper was transported to the sample solution to Paper@QDs@MIPs at both ends, the goal of simultaneous detection was achieved.

Fluorescence rapid detection of paper chip synthesis characterization: First, use environmental scanning electron microscope to characterize the paper chip from the physical structure. The scanning results of the electron microscope were presented in Fig. S4. Fig. S4
A were SEM images of untreated cellulose paper at different magnifications. We saw that the interior was crisscrossed and had a relatively smooth fiber structure. Fig. S4B and C are the images of Paper@CQDs@MIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs, respectively. The remaining Fig. S4D and E are Paper@CQDs@NIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@NIPs. Compared with Fig. S4A, in these groups, we elucidated that there were obvious modified silicon materials on the fiber structure, which verified the effective modification of silicon materials (TEOS).

Subsequently, the energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) equipped with the SEM was used to analyze the modification results of the paper chip. As illustrated in Fig. S5 A, the C and O elements are the basic components of the paper base. Compared with the blank group, Si element appeared on the Paper@CQDs@MIPs, which was the result of TEOS silanization modification (Fig. S5B). Furthermore, Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs (Fig. S5C) showed not only Si element, but also Cd element, and Cd element was derived from CdTe QDs. These results confirm the successful modification and synthesis of Paper@CQDs@MIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs.

By analyzing FT-IR, information about the state and composition of molecules can be obtained, and the amount of sample is small, the detection is efficient and accurate, and it is often used for the characterization of the analyte. Fig. 2B implies the infrared spectra of Paper@CQDs@MIPs (curve b) and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs (curve c), where curve a is the blank control. Fig. 2B shows 3454 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 were the N-H flexural vibration peaks. Considering that APTES was used to modify the paper base during the experiment and the amino group was grafted, the peaks of curves b and c here were stronger than those of curve a. Near 1074 cm−1 was the Si-O-Si absorption peak, and the Si-O symmetrical stretching vibration peak was 611 cm−1. The absorption peaks of curves b and c were enhanced at the corresponding positions, providing new evidence for the modification of silanized materials (from TEOS) beyond the SEM spectrum. The C-N vibrational absorption peak of the amide bond formed by the carboxyl group and the amino group at 1384 cm−1 promoted the combination of the fluorescent substance and the paper base. Therefore, the enhancement of the absorption peaks of curves b and c proves the grafting of CQDs and CdTe QDs. The above results offered evidence of the successful modification and synthesis of paper chips.

The properties of Paper@QDs@MIPs: Generally speaking, QDs have good stability and multiple excitations for a long time have little influence on their fluorescence intensity [11]. Based on this, the fluorescence stability of the paper chips prepared by CQDs and CdTe QDs was measured, and the results are shown in Fig. S2B. The fluorescence intensity of either didn't change significantly under the continuous excitation for 1 h, indicating that the QDs retain their fluorescence stability after being made into paper chips. Furthermore, the thermal stability measurement results of Paper@QDs@MIPs are shown in Fig. 2, where Fig. 2C shows the TG and DTG curves of Paper@CQDs@MIPs, and Fig. 2D shows the TG and DTG curves of Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs. The results point out that the trends of the two paper chips are similar, and they are very stable within 0 ∼ 30℃. When the temperature exceeded 30℃, moisture loss could cause a slight quality loss, but the quality of the paper chips remained basically stable until about 300℃. In the following 100℃ range, the degradation rate of the paper chip reached the fastest rate and then gradually became flat. These results justify that the two kinds of Paper@QDs@MIPs have reliable thermal stability within 290℃ and good thermal stability at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence performance of imprinted Paper chips
ce:section.ce:para: Rapid detection has certain requirements for detection time [39]. Therefore, the paper chip's response time to the template solution is an important characterization for rapid detection. Simultaneously, after the template is added, the fluorescence change of the paper chip within a certain period of time can also reflect the response relationship between the sensor and the template. The fluorescence intensity of the paper chip within 1 h after adding a certain concentration of different templates was tracked, and the results are reported in Fig. S6. The degree of fluorescence quenching reached 47% within 5 min after adding difenoconazole to Paper@CQDs@MIPs (Fig. S6A). However, mancozeb had almost no effect on Paper@CQDs@MIPs (Fig. S6B). On the contrary, after the two templates were added separately, the fluorescence intensity of Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs changed little within 1 h after adding difenoconazole, showing a continuous and slow decline trend (Fig. S6C). Combined with the analysis of the fluorescence stability of the paper chip (Fig. S2B), the decrease in fluorescence intensity here might be quenched by multiple excitations. However, the fluorescence quenching degree of Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs reached 44% after adding mancozeb for 5 min (Fig. S6D). In summary, Paper@CQDs@MIPs were responsive to difenoconazole but not to mancozeb, while Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs responded to mancozeb but had poor response to difenoconazole, which demonstrates the feasibility of this experiment. Altogether, it is clear that the detection time was 5 min, which meets the requirements of rapid detection.

After measuring the response time of Paper@CQDs@MIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs to difenoconazole and mancozeb, respectively, the general trend of the fluorescence intensity of the paper chip was also obtained. Afterward, laser confocal imaging was used to determine the response effect of Paper@QDs@MIPs to the concentration of the imprinting template. As exhibited in Fig. 3, after adding a single template solution with a concentration of 0, 0.5, 1, 4, and 10 μg/mL, the responses of Paper@CQDs@MIPs to different concentrations of difenoconazole were A-E, and the responses of Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs to different concentrations of mancozeb were F-J. The fluorescence intensity of the two kinds of Paper@QDs@MIPs decreased with the increase in template concentration, which corroborates the possibility of detecting mancozeb and difenoconazole.

Generally, the quenching of fluorescent probes can be divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching, its quenching mechanism can be divided into internal filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) according to the different electron transfer of fluorescent substances [40-42], which can be determined by fluorescence lifetime, UV–vis absorption and spectral overlap [43,44]. Fig. 4A and D showed the time-resolved fluorescence spectra of CQDs and CdTe QDs before and after exposure to difenoconazole and mancozeb. The fluorescence lifetime data of quantum dots was obtained by fitting the measurement results of the double exponential equation [39], and the average lifetime calculation formula was as follows:
(2)
τ =
A 1
τ 1
2
+
A 2
τ 2
2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

By fitting the data, the fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs were basically the same and remained around 8.98 ns before and after exposure to difenoconazole. However, the fluorescence lifetime of CeTd QDs decreased significantly, from 32.45 ns to 23.91 ns after exposure to mancozeb. Subsequently, the UV absorption spectra of CQDs and CeTd QDs before and after exposure to difenoconazole and mancozeb were measured, and the results are shown in Fig. 4B and E. After exposure to difenoconazole, in addition to absorption peaks of itself and CQDs, a new fluorescence absorption occurred at about 370 nm, which was presumed to be a non-fluorescent complex formed between difenoconazole and CQDs. However, there was no significant difference in the UV absorption of CeTd QDs before and after exposure to mancozeb. In addition, we also found a large overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of CQDs and the UV absorption spectrum of difenoconazole. At the same time, there was no overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of CeTd QDs and the UV absorption spectrum of mancozeb, as shown in Fig. 4C and F.

In addition, on the grounds of the changes in fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs and CdTe QDs after exposure to difenoconazole and mancozeb, the fluorescence emission peak of CQDs did not shift significantly, while the fluorescence emission peak of CdTe QDs showed blue shift. After that, the fluorescence quenching data were also analyzed by the Stern–Volmer equation.
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
C q
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
C q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of QDs in the absence and presence of quenchers, Ksv and Kq is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, Cq is the concentration of the quencher, and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of QDs.

The quench curve in Fig. 5 shows a good linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching degree of QDs and the concentration of two kinds of pesticides, and their analysis results according to the linear equation are shown in Table S1. The quenching constant Kq of CQDs was 9.3356 × 104 L/mol greater than the maximum diffusion collision quenching constant of biological macromolecules (2 × 1010 L/mol/s). Similarly, the quenching constant Kq of CdTe QDs was 2.8561 × 109 L/mol less than it.

According to the above results, we can conclude that the quenching of CQDs caused by difenoconazole is static quenching, and there may be an IFE mechanism. Furthermore, the quenching of CeTd QDs induced by mancozeb is dynamic quenching, and its mechanism can be explained by the effect of PET.

With regards to CdTe QDs, after exposure to mancozeb, the main reason for the decrease in the average lifetime of CdTe QDs was the electron transfer to mancozeb. By comparing the average lifetime of CdTe QDs before and after exposure to mancozeb, Ket is estimated as follows, and the calculation result is shown in Table S1.
(4)
K
et
=
1
τ
a v 0
-
1
τ
av

As for CQDs, there might be a binding site between carbon CQDs and difenoconazole, which could be extrapolated from the phase interaction between them. The relationship between fluorescence quenching degree of CQDs and concentration of difenoconazole can be expressed as follows:
(5)
l g
F 0
- F
F
= l g
K a
+ n l g
C q
where Ka is the binding constant of CQDs and difenoconazole, L/mol; n is the number of binding sites between them, and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of QDs in the absence and presence of quenchers.

The relation curves of lg[(F0-F)/F] and lgCq are shown in Fig. S7. The number of binding sites of CQDs and difenoconazole was close to 1, indicating only one binding site between them. Furthermore, Ka reached 5.7015 × 104 L/mol (Table S1), indicating that the two reacted rapidly and formed stable compounds, which further verifies that the fluorescence quenching that occurred in them is static quenching.

Finally, in order to further explore the mechanism of its action, molecular simulation was carried out by Material Studio 2019 software. Dmol 3 tool was used to calculate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) as well as the geometric optimization structure of various substances, in which the synthetic raw material folic acid represented CQDs and modified thioglycolic acid represented CdTe QDs. Fig. S8 shows the optimized geometric structures of difenoconazole, folic acid, mancozeb, and thioglycolic acid. Fig. 4G exhibits the HOMO, LUMO, and electron cloud distribution of the four substances, which might support the subsequent theoretical analysis of interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of Dual-channel microfluidic Paper chip
ce:section.ce:para: This experiment aimed to realize the simultaneous rapid detection of two pesticides, difenoconazole and mancozeb. As a result, paper chips were required to achieve effective detection in the mixed solution. To achieve it, a mixed template was added to the paper chip for detection. The results are reported in Fig. 5. For mixed templates, Paper@CQDs@MIPs and Paper@CdTe QDs@MIPs still showed a certain concentration response (A, C) with good linearity (B, D) and their limits of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) were 0.18 and 2 μg/mL respectively. Thus, effective detection of two pesticide residues was achieved. Nevertheless, the effect of Paper@QDs@NIPs was obviously poor (Fig. S9), which clarifies that the Paper@QDs@MIPs we prepared have good practicability and specificity. The microfluidic paper chip was compared with the reported detection methods for difenoconazole and mancozeb. The simultaneous and rapid detection of these two pesticides was easily achieved, so the newly prepared microfluidic paper chip appears to be the ideal detection sensor for simultaneous rapid detection (Table S2).

Moreover, to explore the selectivity of this dual-channel microfluidic paper chip, we tested three compounds that were structurally similar to difenoconazole and mancozeb, including carbendazim, propiconazole, and penconazole. Their chemical structures and fluorescence response results are shown in Fig. 5E. In contrast, Paper@CQDs@MIPs exhibited significant fluorescence quenching effect on difenoconazole with good selectivity but no corresponding response to other analogs. This can be mainly attributed to the recognition cavity that was complementary to the molecular structure of difenoconazole and formed during the molecular imprinting process. Furthermore, the selectivity results for mancozeb were also similar. These results show that the sensor can specifically detect difenoconazole and mancozeb simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical applications of Dual-channel microfluidic Paper chip
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate its application in the detection of real samples, the prepared dual-channel microfluidic paper chip was used to detect difenoconazole and mancozeb in cucumber samples. Samples were spiked at three concentration levels to obtain mean recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD). The results are listed in Table 1 . The recoveries of difenoconazole and mancozeb were similar to the standard of addition, indicating that the prepared microfluidic paper chip can be used for actual food samples. The recovery of cucumber samples spiked with difenoconazole was 102.2–108.8%, and its RSD was 4.7–5.6%. The recovery of cucumber samples spiked with mancozeb was 96.0–105.5% with RSD of 4.8–5.6%. In addition, the experiments indicate that the results obtained by this method are in good agreement with those obtained by HPLC (Table 1). As a matter of fact, the dual-channel and dual-signal microfluidic paper chip can achieve wider application potential for the practical detection of difenoconazole and mancozeb in real food samples.

In this work, fluorescent sensor based on QDs and microfluidic technology, the newly synthesized CQDs and CdTe QDs were used as fluorescence signals with difenoconazole and mancozeb as templates to prepare a new dual-channel and dual-signal microfluidic paper chip. The rapid quantitative detection of the target pesticide could be achieved under the same excitation wavelength within 5 min. The strategy implemented shows the characteristics of portability, fast response, high accuracy, good reproducibility in analysis, and good practical value when applied to actual food samples, which contributes to a research basis for the development of rapid and specific detection methods for a variety of common pesticides. Its application in field detection is conducive to ensuring food safety.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by research grants including the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31701705 and 31972183), the Shaanxi Province Key Research and Development Project (2021NY-145), the Xi’an Advanced Manufacturing Technology and Key Project (21XJZZ0008), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFF1102200), and the Fundamental Research Fund of the Central University (GK202206038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108674.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
